content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree, check whether it is a structural mirror of itself (i.e., structurally symmetric around its center).

For example, the following binary tree is structurally symmetric:


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the single line, `YES` if the given binary tree is structurally symmetric to itself, `NO` otherwise.
constraints: All node ids are distinct within a test case
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the binary tree. The next N‑1 lines each contain three space‑separated values pi ci Ri, where pi is the parent node id, ci is the child node id, and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi). The tree is guaranteed to be a valid binary tree.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the tree is structurally symmetric (a mirror of itself) or "NO" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The tree is perfectly symmetric: left subtree of node 1 mirrors the right subtree.
Both sub‑trees of the root have the same shape, therefore the whole tree is symmetric.
A root with one left and one right child is symmetric regardless of the values.

solution.approach: A binary tree is symmetric if the left subtree is a mirror image of the right subtree. Starting from the root, we recursively compare the left child of the left subtree with the right child of the right subtree and the right child of the left subtree with the left child of the right subtree. If at any point one side is null while the other is not, the tree is not symmetric. The algorithm works in O(N) time per test case because each node is visited once, and uses O(H) auxiliary stack space where H is the height of the tree (worst‑case O(N)).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) for all test cases combined.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the tree (hash map of nodes) plus O(H) recursion stack.
